skill.name: enact-docs-guide
skill.description: Guide for creating, running, publishing, and documenting Enact tools for AI agents.
skill.summary: Create and publish Enact tools with structured inputs, outputs, secrets, and runtime commands.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives caused by Markdown examples and inline code in SKILL.md. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malware behavior, or actual code execution was found. The remaining concern is that the guide documents shell command parameter substitution, which can lead to command injection in tools created from the guidance if inputs are not handled safely.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Shell Parameter Substitution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The guide presents command templates that pass substituted input parameters into shell commands. This is not executed by the skill itself, but tools created from this guidance could become vulnerable if untrusted input is inserted without escaping or argument separation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented templates clearly use substituted parameters in command strings. The skill is documentation-only, so the concern is unsafe downstream guidance rather than direct execution.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur inside fenced code blocks and inline documentation that explain how to use Enact. No executable script, hook, or runtime code in this skill invokes these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious command text is visible only inside Markdown examples. There is no evidence of a file that runs these examples.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable References Are Secret-Handling Documentation
low_findings.description: The API_KEY and environment variable references describe how users should configure secrets for Enact tools. The skill does not read environment variables, access a .env file, or transmit secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are YAML and prose examples for declaring and accessing secrets. No code path performs environment access in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words such as description, sign, secret, and source in documentation. No hashing, encryption, credential harvesting, or system reconnaissance implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are metadata, secret field descriptions, and checklist text. They do not contain cryptographic code or reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Strings With Substituted Parameters
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples use command strings with substituted parameters. This pattern requires careful escaping in real tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented in command examples. It is a downstream injection risk if copied into tools that process untrusted input.

content.value_statement: Teams need concise guidance for building Enact tools that AI agents can run reliably. This skill explains tool structure, commands, schemas, secrets, publishing, and common language templates.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required Enact tool directory structure and enact.md metadata.
Shows common Enact CLI commands for running, installing, searching, signing, and publishing tools.
Provides YAML templates for input schemas, output schemas, runtime images, build steps, and commands.
Summarizes examples for Python, Node.js, Rust, Go, and shell-based tools.
Describes secret declaration with API keys and access through environment variables.
Distinguishes container tools from instruction-only tools.
limitations: It is a documentation guide and does not create files by itself.
It does not validate Enact manifests or run Enact commands.
It does not verify whether a published tool is secure or correct.
Command substitution examples require careful handling for untrusted input.

use_cases.title: Build a First Enact Tool
use_cases.description: Use the guide to understand the minimum files, metadata, command, and schema fields for a new tool.

use_cases.title: Prepare Tool Documentation
use_cases.description: Use the template and checklist to document inputs, outputs, runtime settings, and discovery tags.

use_cases.title: Review Tool Publishing Steps
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to confirm testing, authentication, signing, and publishing steps before release.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Enact Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-docs-guide to explain what an Enact tool is and list the files I need for a minimal tool.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Tool Manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-docs-guide to draft an enact.md outline for a Python tool with one string input and one JSON output.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Runtime Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-docs-guide to compare the Python, Node.js, Rust, Go, and shell examples for my tool idea.

prompt_templates.title: Review Release Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-docs-guide to review my Enact tool plan for schemas, secrets, runtime command, testing, signing, and publishing steps.

output_examples.input: Explain the minimum structure for an Enact tool.
output_examples.output: A concise summary of the required enact.md file, optional source files, metadata fields, and command behavior.

output_examples.input: Help me plan secret handling for an API tool.
output_examples.output: Guidance to declare an API key as a secret, store it outside .env files, and access it through environment variables.

output_examples.input: Check whether my tool is ready to publish.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering name format, description, schemas, runtime image, build steps, command, testing, signing, and publishing.

best_practices: Use pinned runtime images and explicit build commands for repeatable tools.
Define input and output schemas before writing the command implementation.
Store API keys as Enact secrets and avoid placing secrets in source files.
anti_patterns: Do not publish a tool without testing it with representative input.
Do not rely on latest runtime images for production tools.
Do not insert untrusted input into shell commands without safe argument handling.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps you understand and document Enact tools that AI agents can run with structured inputs and outputs.

faq.question: Does it execute Enact commands?
faq.answer: No. It explains commands and workflows, but it does not run the Enact CLI by itself.

faq.question: Can it help with secret configuration?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to declare secrets and access them through environment variables in tool code.

faq.question: Which programming languages are covered?
faq.answer: The guide includes examples for Python, Node.js, Rust, Go, and shell commands.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. It is documentation-only, but command substitution examples need careful handling.

faq.question: What should I verify before publishing a tool?
faq.answer: Check the name, description, schemas, runtime image, build steps, command, JSON output, signing, and publishing workflow.
